title: Docker Port Mapping Validator with Incorrect Port Range

statement.markdown: Write a function that validates Docker port mappings. A valid port mapping is one where the host port is greater than or equal to 0 and less than 65536, and the container port is an integer between 0 and 32767. Your function should return True if the port mapping is valid, False otherwise. Note that the port mapping is represented as an object with two properties: hostPort and containerPort.
statement.inputFormat: A JSON object containing the port mapping as an object with two properties: hostPort and containerPort.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the port mapping is valid.
statement.notes: The port range is defined by the Docker documentation.

constraints.description: The host port of the port mapping
The container port of the port mapping

examples.explanation: The host port is outside the valid range
Both host and container ports are valid
The container port is outside the valid range

hints.text: Verify the host port is within the valid range.
Verify the container port is within the valid range.

editorial.title: Docker Port Mapping Validator
editorial.intuition: A simple validation function that checks both host and container ports.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking each possible value within the valid ranges.
editorial.optimalSolution: A single if statement that checks both host and container ports.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The function returns True if both ports are within the valid ranges, and False otherwise.
editorial.dryRun: Example usage of the function with valid and invalid port mappings.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly assuming a port is valid without checking the range.
